claims.claim: We evaluate whether batching Hugging Face extractive-reader calls across a mixed-domain benchmark (HotpotQA and SQuAD) preserves the quality and token-savings profile of the Query-Evidence Truncation Strategy (QETS).

evidence_refs.quote: # Batched Reader QETS Mixed-Domain Scaling Validation

claims.notes: Historical backfill linked the claim to already published artifacts; this is not independent source revalidation.

claims.claim: On a 320-example mixed-domain sample (200 HotpotQA, 120 SQuAD) at a QETS budget ratio of 0.65 and reader batch size 16, we observe 46.3% overall prompt-token reduction with a +0.015 percentage-point answer-F1 delta, +0.625 percentage-point exact-match delta, and a −2.34 percentage-point support-recall delta relative to the vanilla (non-truncated) reader.

evidence_refs.quote: # Batched Reader QETS Mixed-Domain Scaling Validation

claims.notes: Historical backfill linked the claim to already published artifacts; this is not independent source revalidation.

claims.claim: QETS reader throughput increased from 37.0 packs/s at batch size 1 to 59.0 packs/s at batch size 16.

evidence_refs.quote: # Batched Reader QETS Mixed-Domain Scaling Validation

claims.notes: Historical backfill linked the claim to already published artifacts; this is not independent source revalidation.

claims.claim: The branch kill condition—finalizing negatively if batching materially degrades quality or if token savings fall below 15% at non-negative answer F1—was not triggered.

claims.claim: Query-Evidence Truncation Strategy (QETS) reduces prompt-token consumption in extractive question-answering pipelines by selecting and truncating evidence chunks before reader invocation, guided by a reader-aware relevance formula.

claims.claim: Prior work in the parent project (project `source-record-redacted`) validated QETS on single-domain benchmarks (HotpotQA and SQuAD separately) using sequential reader calls.

claims.claim: Batching is desirable for throughput but introduces a risk: batched inference may alter numerical precision, padding behavior, or attention patterns in ways that shift predictions relative to the sequential baseline.

evidence_refs.quote: # Batched Reader QETS Mixed-Domain Scaling Validation

claims.notes: Historical backfill linked the claim to already published artifacts; this is not independent source revalidation.

claims.claim: This successor project tests the joint mixed-domain + batched-reader scaling hypothesis under a pre-registered kill condition: finalize negatively if batching materially changes predictions or quality versus the sequential reader, or if mixed-domain QETS fails to retain at least 15% token savings at non-negative answer F1 on the calibrated sample.
